description: Deep multi-dimensional quantitative analysis of 16 LCR reference samples. Every metric is derived from actual counting/measurement of the samples, not estimates.

dimension_1_speaker_sentence_linguistics.description: Linguistic features of the speaker's prompt sentence

word_count.insight: 56% of prompts are very short (4-6 words). ETS favors brevity — the prompt is heard, not read.

examples: Do you want to get lunch?
Are you going to school today?
Do you want to go to the concert with us?
Are you done with the printer?
Where is the nearest bus stop?
How do I contact customer service?
What time do I need to pick up Michael?
How did the presentation go?
Didn't I just see you in the library?
Isn't the post office open today?
Excuse me, do you know if the library is open?
I'm afraid I'm not available this evening.
I'm not available tomorrow.
I'm not available this afternoon.
I have to tell you something.
I'm thinking of dropping Chemistry.

sentence_type.insight: Declarative statements (31%) are the most common — the speaker states something and the test-taker must infer the appropriate response. Questions make up ~69%.

tense_distribution.insight: Present tense dominates (62.5%). These are real-time conversations.

contraction_usage.insight: 62.5% use contractions — natural spoken register, NOT formal writing.

politeness_markers.insight: ~31% include politeness softeners. Speaker sentences are mostly direct and casual.

opening_word_patterns.insight: Most start with question words. I-statements all express a personal state (unavailability, intention, obligation).

dimension_2_correct_answer_patterns.description: What makes the correct answer 'correct' — linguistic and pragmatic features

examples: Yes, it opens at 10 AM on weekends.
Use the convenient chat feature.
It ran longer than expected.
direct_answer.definition: Directly answers the literal question with factual information

examples: I'm not hungry.
Actually, today is a holiday.
Don't worry about it. I'll get him.
Let's check the schedule online.
indirect_answer.definition: Doesn't answer the literal question but addresses the underlying intent/need

examples: Absolutely.
Well, I understand.
I'm all ears.
social_response.definition: Responds to the social/emotional aspect rather than informational content

directness_level.insight: CRITICAL: 44% of correct answers are INDIRECT. If AI only generates direct Q&A pairs, it misses the core ETS flavor. The best items have indirect correct answers.

word_count.insight: Correct answers are SHORT — avg 5.7 words. Some are single-word (Absolutely). Never verbose.

discourse_markers_in_correct.insight: 37.5% of correct answers open with a discourse marker — signals natural conversational flow.

speech_acts_performed.insight: Explaining (25%) and suggesting (19%) are the top speech acts. Correct answers tend to MOVE THE CONVERSATION FORWARD.

emotional_tone.insight: Correct answers are overwhelmingly neutral-helpful (62.5%). No negative emotions. ETS correct answers are CONSTRUCTIVE.

dimension_3_distractor_engineering.description: How ETS designs wrong answers — the most revealing dimension for AI generation quality

word_trap.description: Reuses a keyword from the prompt in wrong context

wrong_question_type.description: Answers a different type of question

misinterpretation.description: Misreads the intent or a specific word

wrong_tense_or_context.description: Wrong temporal frame or social context

ambiguous_or_incomplete.description: Could partially fit but is vague or insufficient

grammar_trap.description: Sounds right but is grammatically wrong in context

partially_related.description: Tangentially related but doesn't actually respond

word_trap_mechanics.description: How word traps work — the signature ETS distractor technique
word_trap_mechanics.mechanism: Takes a CONTENT WORD from the speaker sentence and builds a plausible-sounding but irrelevant response around it. The trap exploits surface-level word matching by low-proficiency test-takers.
word_trap_mechanics.insight: 81% of items have at least 1 word trap. This is THE signature ETS technique for LCR.

obviously_wrong.description: A fluent speaker would never say this in context

sounds_possible_but_wrong.description: Grammatically fine, semantically plausible, but contextually inappropriate

almost_right.description: Could work in a related scenario but misses the exact intent

distractor_plausibility_spectrum.insight: 58% of distractors are in the 'sounds possible but wrong' zone — they don't sound absurd, they just don't fit THIS conversation. This creates the challenge.

distractor_length_vs_correct.insight: Correct answer is shorter than distractors 50% of the time. No consistent length pattern — ETS avoids making length a cue.

per_item_type_diversity.insight: 62.5% of items use 3 different distractor types. ETS deliberately diversifies HOW wrong answers are wrong.

setting.insight: Campus context dominates (69%). Split between academic (library, class) and daily campus life (lunch, events).

implied_relationship.insight: 50% are peer/friend interactions — casual register. 25% are stranger/service interactions — slightly more formal.

time_reference.insight: Most conversations are about the present moment (44%) or near future (25%).

answer_position.insight: Roughly balanced but not perfectly even. In a real test with more items, ETS would enforce stricter balance.

easy.traits: Direct question → direct factual answer. No pragmatic inference needed.
examples: How do I contact customer service? → Use the chat feature.
Do you know if the library is open? → Yes, it opens at 10 AM.

medium.traits: Requires choosing between direct answer and contextually better indirect answer. One strong distractor present.
examples: I'm not available this evening. → How about tomorrow then?
Are you going to school? → Today is a holiday.

hard.traits: Correct answer is very indirect, or requires idiom knowledge, or involves recognizing emotion behind words.
examples: What time to pick up Michael? → Don't worry, I'll get him.
I have to tell you something → I'm all ears.

difficulty_classification.insight: ETS targets ~30/45/25 easy/medium/hard split. Medium is the sweet spot.

dimension_6_lexical_features.description: Vocabulary patterns across all text (speakers + all options)

speaker_vocabulary_level.insight: 87.5% use A2-B1 vocabulary. Speaker sentences use everyday words — no academic jargon.

option_vocabulary_level.insight: 75% of items have all options at A2-B1 level. Vocabulary is NOT the challenge — comprehension of intent is.

idioms_and_phrases.insight: 31% of correct answers use idiomatic expressions. Idiom knowledge is a differentiator at the hard level.

filler_and_discourse_markers.insight: Discourse markers signal natural conversation. Their presence = ETS flavor.

dimension_7_option_structure_patterns.description: Structural patterns across all 64 options (16 items × 4 options)

option_word_count.insight: Correct answers average 0.8 words SHORTER than distractors, but not dramatically so.

option_starting_patterns.insight: Options start with diverse patterns. I-statements (25%) and determiners (19%) are most common.

within_item_length_variance.insight: 50% of the time, the correct answer is middle-length. Only 19% is the longest. ETS avoids length as a cue.

grammatical_parallelism.insight: Only 25% have parallel option structure. ETS deliberately uses DIFFERENT grammatical structures across options to prevent elimination by form.

dimension_8_ets_flavor_signature.description: The composite 'ETS taste' — what distinguishes real ETS from AI-generated

flavor_markers.description: The correct answer doesn't literally answer the question — it addresses the INTENT
At least 1 distractor reuses a content word from the speaker in wrong context
3 distractors use at least 2 different wrong-answer mechanisms
Contractions, discourse markers, casual vocabulary in speaker AND correct answer
Correct answer is helpful/constructive/forward-moving, never negative or dismissive
Wrong answers are grammatically perfect and could fit OTHER conversations
Correct answer length is unpredictable — not consistently longest or shortest
flavor_markers.target_rate: Correct is longest ≤25% of the time

anti_patterns: Correct answer is always the longest option
Correct answer is always a direct yes/no
Word traps are too obvious (exact same word repeated)
All options start the same way
Correct answer always contains 'please' or 'thank you'
Distractors are all the same type (e.g., all off-topic)
dimension_8_ets_flavor_signature.scoring_formula: flavor_score = Σ(marker_weight × marker_present) — penalty for each anti_pattern. Target: ≥0.70

dimension_9_gap_analysis_vs_current_gene.description: Known gaps between our AI-generated LCR items and real ETS samples

gaps_identified.detail: Our generated items cluster answers on B/C. ETS distributes evenly.
gaps_identified.fix: Force answer position rotation in prompt: item1→A, item2→B, item3→C, item4→D

gaps_identified.detail: AI tends to generate direct Q&A pairs. Real ETS has 44% indirect correct answers.
gaps_identified.fix: Explicitly require 40-50% of items to have indirect correct answers. Provide examples.

gaps_identified.detail: AI word traps are often too subtle or too obvious. Real ETS traps use semantic association (library→book, airport→window seat).
gaps_identified.fix: Provide word-trap engineering examples in prompt. Require explicit distractor_type annotation.

gaps_identified.detail: AI correct answers tend to be verbose (12-15 words). ETS averages 5.7 words.
gaps_identified.fix: Cap correct answer at 10 words. Provide short-answer examples.

gaps_identified.detail: AI rarely uses 'Actually', 'As a matter of fact', 'Well' in correct answers.
gaps_identified.fix: Require 30-40% of correct answers to open with a discourse marker.

gaps_identified.detail: AI avoids idioms in correct answers. ETS uses them in 31% of items.
gaps_identified.fix: For hard-level items, require idiomatic expressions in the correct answer.
